chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning SAM patients from F-75 to F-100 during rehabilitation focus on safety, catch-up growth monitoring, and prevention of refeeding syndrome.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for transitioning from stabilization to rehabilitation feeding in SAM management.
Learn the structured approach to replacing F-75 with F-100 and monitoring for cardiac intolerance.
Identify and manage complications such as refeeding syndrome during catch-up growth.
Interpret weight gain metrics and assess for underlying issues in poor growth outcomes.
key_facts: Transition to F-100 begins when appetite returns, temperature stabilizes, hypoglycemia resolves, and oedema reduces or resolves.
Daily weight gain is categorized as good (≥10 g/kg/day), moderate (5-<10 g/kg/day), and poor (<5 g/kg/day).
Refeeding syndrome prevention includes phased feeding (F-75 to F-100/RUTF), early potassium and magnesium repletion, and electrolyte monitoring.
Monitor for cardiac intolerance signs such as tachypnea, rising pulse, hepatomegaly, or recurrent oedema during rehabilitation feeding.
Indian guidelines suggest replacing F-75 with equal volumes of F-100 for 2 days, then gradually increasing feed amounts.
reference_quote: Begin rehabilitation only when appetite has returned, temperature is stable, there has been no further hypoglycemia, and oedema is reducing or resolved. (Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Transition from F-75 to F-100 feeding
